title: Project Overview
description: Start with the README to understand what llama Mail is: a self-hosted IMAP web client that polls unread mail and auto-applies keyword labels using a local Ollama model, all wrapped in a single-container deployment. The backend module guide (backend/AGENTS.md) complements it by laying out the Go package structure, the HTTP API contract, and the per-user multi-tenant data model you will meet throughout this tour. Read these first to build a mental map before touching code.

title: Entry Point & Process Model
description: The binary starts in main.go, which runs a one-time config migration and then hands off to the app package's Run orchestrator. app.go is the composition root: it loads configuration, constructs the user store, llama client, poller, health monitor, and HTTP server, then runs the polling daemon and the API server together in one process (dispatched by --mode). This is the single most important file for seeing how every subsystem is wired together.
languageLesson: In Go, a project's runnable command lives under cmd/ and stays thin, delegating real work to internal/ packages. The internal/ directory is special: Go's compiler forbids importing it from outside the module, enforcing a clean public/private boundary.

title: Configuration Model
description: Building on the bootstrap in Step 2, config.go defines the settings model and loader covering llama, scan, rate-limit, redaction, label, and notification options, plus VAPID and notification key material. LoadOrInit reads or creates the config on disk and generates key material on first run, so nearly every subsystem depends on this file (fan-in of 6). Understanding the config shape here makes the rest of the backend legible.

title: The HTTP API Server
description: server.go is the central API surface (the highest fan-out file in the codebase), wiring routes for IMAP config, mail send/draft/attachments, inbox actions, notifications, desktop pairing, auth with TOTP/recovery MFA, tuning, logs, and serving the frontend. Because llama Mail is multi-tenant, server_userscope.go resolves per-user config/state directories and lazily builds each user's own state, contacts, mail-cache, and IMAP client instances. Together they show how one server safely isolates many users' mailboxes.

title: The Classification Pipeline (Heart of the App)
description: poller.go is the core background daemon and the reason this project exists: for each user it polls the IMAP inbox, redacts sensitive text, classifies unread messages via the llama client, applies IMAP keyword labels, and dispatches notifications with rate limiting and health tracking. The Ollama HTTP client warms up and pulls models, paces and retries classification calls, and parses the model's output against the allowed label set. redact.go scrubs sensitive patterns before any text reaches the model or the logs. This is the classification flow the README describes, in code.
languageLesson: The llama package splits a narrow interface (client.go's SelectLabelFromText) from its concrete HTTP implementation (http_client.go). This adapter pattern lets the poller depend on an abstraction, keeping the AI backend swappable and testable.

title: Prompt Tuning Contract
description: TUNING.md is the prompt-tuning template that defines the allowed labels in priority order, the classification rules, per-label triggers, and the exact input/output contract the model must follow. The HTTP client from Step 5 loads this tuning text and validates model output against these labels, so this document is effectively executable configuration. Reading it clarifies why the classifier behaves the way it does and how an operator steers labeling without touching code.

title: IMAP Integration & Mail Cache
description: The IMAP adapter connects to a user's mail server and implements mailbox listing, message and overview fetching, label and folder management, inbox actions, attachment retrieval, and draft/sent saving, loading encrypted stored credentials via cryptutil. The mail cache store persists a mutex-guarded snapshot keyed by mailbox window and delta-syncs against live IMAP data, so the UI and poller avoid re-fetching everything each tick. Outbound mail is assembled by mailmsg.go into a MIME message for SMTP delivery.

title: Persistence, State & Encryption
description: state/store.go is the per-user persistent store backing checkpoints, processed-message tracking, classification decisions, push subscriptions, AI-credit status, and desktop pairing codes on disk (fan-in of 7). cryptutil.go provides the symmetric seal/open used to protect stored IMAP and CardDAV credentials, while fsutil.go supplies the atomic temp-file-plus-rename writes that every store relies on for crash safety (the highest fan-in utility). This layer is how a stateless-looking daemon remembers what it already did.
languageLesson: Atomic writes via write-to-temp-then-rename exploit the OS guarantee that rename() is atomic on the same filesystem, so a reader never sees a half-written file even if the process crashes mid-write.

title: Auth & Multi-Factor Authentication
description: users.go is the account store handling load/migration, roles, activation, scrypt password hashing, and TOTP/push MFA state. totp.go is a self-contained time-based one-time-password implementation, and mfa.go manages the in-memory challenge store for TOTP attempts, push approvals, and recovery codes. The MFA handlers expose setup, confirm, disable, and recovery-code endpoints, gated behind password confirmation, tying the crypto primitives to the HTTP API from Step 4.
languageLesson: TOTP (RFC 6238) derives a short-lived code by HMAC-ing the current time step against a shared secret. The drift window lets validation accept the adjacent time steps, tolerating small clock skew between server and authenticator app.

title: Push Notifications: Backend Dispatch
description: push_dispatch.go fans out web push to browser subscriptions and native push to registered devices, tracking sent/failed/removed outcomes, while native_sender.go delivers FCM/APNs notifications through a relay Worker, handling relay-key resolution, device registration, and stale-response detection. The same native dispatch path is reused for push-based MFA approvals in push_mfa_handlers.go, which enrolls approver devices and polls challenge responses. This is the backend half of the notification story that began in the poller.

title: Push Notifications: Cloudflare Worker Relays
description: Because a self-hosted box cannot hold Apple/Google push credentials safely, delivery is proxied through two Cloudflare Workers. The FCM and APNs relay entry points authenticate senders, enforce rate limits, and route /send, /register, and /admin/keys, delegating actual delivery to the FCM and APNs transport layers that mint and cache signed provider JWTs. push-relay-common.ts is the shared core both Workers use for API-key minting, admin auth, rate limiting, and box self-registration. This subsystem is what native_sender.go from Step 10 talks to.
languageLesson: Cloudflare Workers run on the V8 isolate model, not Node.js: there is no filesystem and state lives in bindings like KV. Provider tokens (FCM OAuth2, APNs ES256 JWT) are cached in-memory per isolate and refreshed on staleness to avoid re-signing on every request.

title: Frontend Single-Page App
description: main.tsx applies the stored theme and mounts App.tsx, the root React component that owns session/auth state and top-level tab routing across every admin page plus shared compose/draft state. auth.tsx defines the AuthContext and useAuth hook that gate routes by role, and client.ts is the mandatory thin fetch wrapper every page uses to call the Go backend. ReadPage.tsx is the primary mailbox view, rendering sanitized email HTML and building reply/forward drafts, closing the loop with the mail APIs from Step 4.
languageLesson: React Context (auth.tsx) provides a value to an entire component subtree without prop-drilling; the useAuth hook consumes it. Routing the whole app through a single client.ts fetch wrapper centralizes error handling and auth headers in one place.

title: Infrastructure & Deployment
description: The Dockerfile is a multi-stage build: a Go stage compiles the static llama-lab binary, a Node stage builds the Vite frontend, and a final runtime image bundles the binary, Ollama, and supervisor. supervisord.conf orchestrates the container's processes: bootstrap, the API server, the polling daemon, ollama serve, and the one-shot model pull, matching the process model from Step 2. docker-compose.yml ties it together with named volumes for config, secrets, logs, state, and Ollama models. This is how everything you have seen ships as one container.
languageLesson: Multi-stage Docker builds use several FROM stages so heavy build toolchains (Go compiler, npm) never reach the final image, keeping it small. supervisord is the classic answer to running multiple long-lived processes in one container, since a container starts only a single PID 1.
